content.body: Population ecology studies how and why population sizes change over time. Conservation biology applies ecological principles to protect biodiversity.

Population growth:
Exponential: dN/dt = rN (unlimited resources)
Logistic: dN/dt = rN(K-N)/K
r = intrinsic growth rate, K = carrying capacity, N = population size

Population measurement:
• Mark-recapture: N = (M×C)/R
  M=marked, C=captured second time, R=recaptured marked

content.title: Survivorship Curves
content.body:
Type I (convex): high survival through most of life, death in old age
Examples: humans, elephants

Type II (diagonal): constant mortality rate throughout life
Examples: birds, squirrels

Type III (concave): very high early mortality, survivors live long
Examples: fish, oysters, trees (produce many offspring, most die young)

Conservation strategies:
• In-situ: protected areas, national parks (Uganda: Bwindi, QE)
• Ex-situ: zoos, seed banks, captive breeding
• Legislation: CITES, national laws
• Habitat restoration
• Corridors connecting fragmented habitats
• Community conservation

questions.question: Carrying capacity (K) is
options: maximum sustainable population in given environment
questions.answer: maximum sustainable population in given environment
questions.explanation: K is the maximum population size that an environment can sustainably support given available resources.

questions.question: In logistic growth, the growth rate is maximum when
questions.answer: N=K/2
questions.explanation: Population growth rate (dN/dt) peaks at N=K/2 in the logistic model — the inflection point of the S-curve.

questions.question: Mark-recapture formula N = MC/R estimates
questions.answer: total population size
questions.explanation: Lincoln-Petersen method: N = (marked released × total recaptured) / recaptured that are marked.

questions.question: Type I survivorship curve (humans) indicates
options: high survival to old age then rapid death
questions.answer: high survival to old age then rapid death
questions.explanation: Type I: species that invest heavily in few offspring, most survive to old age (humans, elephants).

questions.question: Type III survivorship (fish, trees) produces many offspring because
options: most offspring die young — only a few survive
questions.answer: most offspring die young — only a few survive
questions.explanation: Type III: high early mortality strategy — produce thousands of offspring so a few survive, despite most dying young.

questions.question: In-situ conservation means
options: conserving species in their natural habitat
questions.answer: conserving species in their natural habitat
questions.explanation: In-situ: protecting species where they naturally live — national parks, nature reserves, marine protected areas.

questions.question: Uganda's Bwindi Impenetrable Forest is important for
questions.answer: mountain gorilla conservation
questions.explanation: Bwindi is home to ~half the world's mountain gorillas — critically endangered great apes protected in this UNESCO site.

questions.question: Habitat fragmentation reduces biodiversity because
options: it creates edge effects and isolates populations
questions.answer: it creates edge effects and isolates populations
questions.explanation: Fragmented habitats reduce species range, prevent gene flow, create more vulnerable edge habitat, and increase extinction risk.

questions.question: CITES is an international agreement that
options: controls international trade in endangered species
questions.answer: controls international trade in endangered species
questions.explanation: CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species) regulates/bans trade in threatened species.

questions.question: Minimum viable population (MVP) is
options: smallest population with >99% chance of survival for 100+ years
questions.answer: smallest population with >99% chance of survival for 100+ years
questions.explanation: MVP is the threshold below which a population faces high extinction risk from genetic, demographic, and environmental factors.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Evolution is the change in inherited characteristics of populations over time. Natural selection is the main mechanism: individuals with advantageous traits reproduce more successfully.

Darwin's theory of natural selection:
1. Variation: individuals differ in heritable traits
2. Overproduction: more offspring produced than survive
3. Competition: struggle for limited resources
4. Selection: individuals with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
5. Inheritance: favourable traits passed to offspring
→ allele frequencies change over generations

content.title: Evidence for Evolution
content.body:
Fossil record: show progression of forms over time
Comparative anatomy: homologous structures (e.g. pentadactyl limb: human arm, whale flipper, bat wing, horse leg all share same bones)
Vestigial structures: reduced non-functional remnants (human tailbone, whale pelvic bones)
DNA/molecular: closely related species have similar DNA sequences
Direct observation: antibiotic resistance, peppered moths

content.title: Types of Natural Selection
content.body:
Directional: one extreme phenotype favoured (bacteria becoming antibiotic resistant)
Stabilising: intermediate phenotype favoured, extremes selected against (human birth weight)
Disruptive: both extremes favoured over intermediate (can lead to speciation)

questions.question: Natural selection requires
questions.answer: heritable variation among individuals
questions.explanation: Without variation, there is nothing for selection to act on. Variation must be heritable to change future generations.

questions.question: The 'survival of the fittest' means
options: organisms best adapted to environment reproduce more
questions.answer: organisms best adapted to environment reproduce more
questions.explanation: 'Fitness' in evolutionary terms = reproductive success. Best-adapted individuals leave more offspring.

questions.question: Antibiotic resistance in bacteria is an example of
questions.answer: directional natural selection
questions.explanation: Antibiotics create selection pressure. Resistant bacteria survive and reproduce → resistant allele frequency increases (directional selection).

questions.question: Homologous structures provide evidence for evolution because
options: same underlying structure in different species suggests common ancestor
they are found in all organisms
questions.answer: same underlying structure in different species suggests common ancestor
questions.explanation: Homologous structures (same bones, different function) indicate descent from a common ancestor with modification.

questions.question: Vestigial structures are
options: remnants of structures that had a function in ancestors
questions.answer: remnants of structures that had a function in ancestors
questions.explanation: Vestigial organs (human appendix, coccyx; whale pelvis) are evolutionary remnants — evidence of common ancestry.

questions.question: Stabilising selection acts on human birth weight because
options: very small and very large babies have higher mortality
questions.answer: very small and very large babies have higher mortality
questions.explanation: Intermediate birth weight (2.5-4kg) has lowest mortality. Very small (premature issues) and very large (delivery problems) have higher risk.

questions.question: Speciation occurs when
options: populations become reproductively isolated and diverge genetically over time
questions.answer: populations become reproductively isolated and diverge genetically over time
questions.explanation: Speciation: populations separated (geography, behaviour, etc.) evolve independently until they can no longer interbreed.

questions.question: Molecular evidence for evolution includes
options: DNA sequences — closely related species have more similar DNA
questions.answer: DNA sequences — closely related species have more similar DNA
questions.explanation: DNA comparison directly measures genetic relatedness. More similar DNA sequences = more recent common ancestor.

questions.question: Allopatric speciation involves
questions.answer: geographical isolation separating populations
questions.explanation: Allopatric: populations separated by geographic barrier (mountain, river, ocean). Isolated populations evolve independently → new species.

questions.question: The peppered moth example shows natural selection because
options: industrial soot darkened trees — dark moths survived predation better, became more common
questions.answer: industrial soot darkened trees — dark moths survived predation better, became more common
questions.explanation: Pre-industrial: pale moths camouflaged on pale trees. After: soot darkened trees, dark moths had better camouflage — frequency of dark allele increased.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane

questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body:
Population growth models:
Exponential: dN/dt = rN (unlimited resources)
Logistic: dN/dt = rN(K-N)/K
K = carrying capacity (max sustainable population)

Population parameters:
Birth rate b, death rate d
r = b - d (intrinsic rate of natural increase)

Survivorship curves:
Type I: most survive to old age (humans, elephants)
Type II: constant death rate (birds)
Type III: high early mortality (fish, insects)

content.title: r and K selection
content.body:
r-selected species: high r, many offspring, little parental care, short lifespan, unstable environments
Examples: bacteria, insects, small rodents

K-selected: low r, few offspring, high parental care, long lifespan, stable environments near K
Examples: elephants, primates, whales

Uganda conservation: Mountain gorillas (K-selected, critically endangered, ~1000 individuals)

content.title: Conservation Strategies
content.body:
IUCN Red List: EX, EW, CR, EN, VU, NT, LC
In situ: protected areas (Queen Elizabeth NP, Bwindi, Murchison Falls)
Ex situ: Uganda Wildlife Education Centre, Entebbe Botanical Gardens
CITES: regulates international trade in threatened species
Resilience: larger, connected habitats — genetic corridors

questions.question: Carrying capacity K is
questions.answer: maximum population sustainable by environment
questions.explanation: K = environmental ceiling for population. When N→K, logistic growth rate → 0

questions.question: Logistic growth differs from exponential because
options: population growth slows as N approaches K (resource limitation)
questions.answer: population growth slows as N approaches K (resource limitation)
questions.explanation: Logistic: S-shaped curve. Growth rate maximum at K/2, approaches zero at K

questions.question: r-selected species produce
options: few offspring with high parental care
many offspring with little parental care — adapted for unstable environments
questions.answer: many offspring with little parental care — adapted for unstable environments
questions.explanation: r-selection: quantity strategy. Insects, annual plants — boom-bust in variable environments

questions.question: Mountain gorillas (CR, ~1000) are K-selected because
options: they produce few offspring, have long lives, slow reproduction — vulnerable to population decline
questions.answer: they produce few offspring, have long lives, slow reproduction — vulnerable to population decline
questions.explanation: K-selected: gorilla females produce 1 infant every 4-5 years. Slow recovery from population decline → vulnerable

questions.question: IUCN Red List category CR means
options: critically endangered — extremely high extinction risk
questions.answer: critically endangered — extremely high extinction risk
questions.explanation: CR: >80% population decline in 10 years OR <250 mature individuals. Facing extremely high extinction risk

questions.question: In situ conservation is preferable to ex situ because
options: preserving species in natural habitat maintains ecological interactions and evolutionary potential
questions.answer: preserving species in natural habitat maintains ecological interactions and evolutionary potential
questions.explanation: Wild populations can evolve and adapt; maintain natural behaviours; contribute to ecosystem function

questions.question: Genetic corridor between habitat fragments helps because
options: it allows gene flow preventing inbreeding and maintaining genetic diversity
questions.answer: it allows gene flow preventing inbreeding and maintaining genetic diversity
questions.explanation: Corridors: allow individuals to move between fragments → random mating → heterozygosity maintained → reduced inbreeding depression

questions.question: Survivorship curve Type III (high early mortality, few reach old age) is typical of
questions.answer: fish and insects
questions.explanation: Type III: large numbers die young (predation, starvation). Survivors have good prospects. Fish produce millions of eggs

questions.question: Sustainable yield harvest takes
options: amount equal to population growth rate (so population maintained)
questions.answer: amount equal to population growth rate (so population maintained)
questions.explanation: Maximum sustainable yield (MSY): harvest equals growth so population doesn't decline. Applied to fisheries, forests

questions.question: Queen Elizabeth National Park in Uganda protects
options: diverse wildlife including tree-climbing lions, hippos, elephants, hundreds of bird species
questions.answer: diverse wildlife including tree-climbing lions, hippos, elephants, hundreds of bird species
questions.explanation: Queen Elizabeth NP: Uganda's most popular park. Famous tree-climbing lions in Ishasha, large hippo populations in Kazinga Channel

questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Ecology Advanced. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Ecology Advanced concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Ecology Advanced builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Ecology Advanced. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Ecology Advanced. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Ecology Advanced include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Ecology Advanced. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Ecology Advanced. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Ecology Advanced concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Ecology Advanced builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Ecology Advanced. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Ecology Advanced. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Ecology Advanced include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Ecology Advanced. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
